cards.definition: the smallest particle of an element that can exist — made up of a nucleus (protons and neutrons) surrounded by electrons
a positively charged particle found in the nucleus of an atom — its number defines the element
a particle with no charge found in the nucleus — its number can vary in isotopes of the same element
a negatively charged particle orbiting the nucleus in shells — involved in chemical bonding
the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom — unique to each element
the total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus of an atom
atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons
a table of all elements arranged in order of atomic number — elements in the same group have similar chemical properties
a vertical column in the periodic table — elements in the same group have the same number of outer electrons and similar properties
a horizontal row in the periodic table — the period number tells you how many electron shells an atom has
bonding formed by the transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal — creates oppositely charged ions that attract each other
bonding formed by the sharing of electrons between non-metal atoms
bonding in metals where positive ions are surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons — explains electrical conductivity and malleability
a compound formed by ionic bonding — forms a giant lattice structure with high melting point
a substance made of small molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces — low melting/boiling points
a substance with many atoms bonded covalently in a large network — e.g. diamond, graphite, silicon dioxide. Very high melting points
a form of carbon with a giant covalent structure — each carbon bonded to four others. Very hard, high melting point, does not conduct electricity
a form of carbon where each atom bonds to three others in layers — layers can slide, making it slippery. Conducts electricity due to delocalised electrons
a long chain molecule made by joining many small monomer units together — e.g. polyethene, PVC
materials with particles between 1 and 100 nanometres in size — have very large surface area to volume ratio and special properties
a reaction that releases energy to the surroundings — temperature of surroundings increases (e.g. combustion, neutralisation)
a reaction that absorbs energy from the surroundings — temperature of surroundings decreases (e.g. thermal decomposition, photosynthesis)
the minimum energy required for reactants to collide and react — a catalyst lowers the activation energy
a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being used up — lowers the activation energy
the loss of electrons, or the gain of oxygen — in terms of oxidation state, oxidation is an increase
the gain of electrons, or the loss of oxygen — in terms of oxidation state, reduction is a decrease
a reaction involving both oxidation and reduction simultaneously — one species loses electrons while another gains them
using electrical energy to break down a compound — positive ions move to the cathode (negative electrode), negative ions to the anode
a substance that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) in solution — pH below 7
a substance that neutralises an acid — an alkali dissolves in water to produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻). pH above 7

topics.name: Topic 6 — Groups in the Periodic Table

cards.definition: very reactive metals (lithium, sodium, potassium etc.) — react vigorously with water, producing hydrogen gas and an alkaline solution
reactive non-metals (fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine) — reactivity decreases down the group
unreactive gases (helium, neon, argon etc.) — have a full outer electron shell so do not need to react
alkali metals become more reactive down the group — outer electron is further from the nucleus and lost more easily
halogens become less reactive down the group — harder to gain an electron as the outer shell is further from the nucleus
a more reactive halogen can displace a less reactive one from its salt solution — e.g. chlorine displaces bromine from potassium bromide solution
metals in the central block of the periodic table — generally harder, denser, and less reactive than Group 1 metals. Form coloured compounds
a test to identify metal ions based on the colour they produce in a flame — e.g. sodium burns yellow, potassium burns lilac
a reaction in which an insoluble solid (precipitate) forms when two solutions are mixed — used to identify ions
a list of metals in order of their reactivity — potassium is most reactive, gold is least reactive
a fossil fuel formed from ancient marine organisms over millions of years — a mixture of hydrocarbons
a compound containing only carbon and hydrogen atoms — crude oil is a mixture of hydrocarbons
the process used to separate crude oil into fractions based on different boiling points
the burning of a fuel in oxygen — complete combustion produces CO₂ and H₂O; incomplete produces CO and soot
gases that absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, warming the atmosphere — CO₂, methane, and water vapour are key examples
the increase in average global temperatures caused by rising levels of greenhouse gases from human activity
the continuous movement of carbon between the atmosphere, living organisms, oceans, and rocks
breaking down large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller, more useful ones using heat and a catalyst
Earth's early atmosphere was mainly carbon dioxide and water vapour — oxygen built up as photosynthetic organisms evolved
an industrial process making ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen — uses an iron catalyst, high pressure, and a temperature of about 450°C
